title: Domain-Driven Design Cheat Sheet
description: Key concepts and patterns in Domain-Driven Design for software engineers.

commands.command: // Use terms that are understood by both developers and domain experts
commands.description: Define a shared language between developers and domain experts
commands.scenario: Define a shared language between developers and domain experts

commands.description: Ensure consistent terminology in code and documentation
commands.scenario: Ensure consistent terminology in code and documentation

commands.command: // Rename 'User' to 'Customer' to better reflect the domain
commands.description: Refactor code to align with domain terms
commands.scenario: Refactor code to align with domain terms

commands.command: // Define a Bounded Context for the Sales domain
commands.description: Define boundaries within which a particular model is applicable
commands.scenario: Define boundaries within which a particular model is applicable

commands.command: // Use different namespaces or modules for different contexts
commands.description: Ensure clear separation between different contexts
commands.scenario: Ensure clear separation between different contexts

commands.command: // Create a context map to show relationships between contexts
commands.description: Integrate between bounded contexts using context maps
commands.scenario: Integrate between bounded contexts using context maps

commands.description: Define an entity with a unique identity
commands.scenario: Define an entity with a unique identity

commands.description: Define a value object that represents a descriptive aspect
commands.scenario: Define a value object that represents a descriptive aspect

commands.command: // Use a UUID for unique identification of entities
commands.description: Ensure entities have a unique identifier
commands.scenario: Ensure entities have a unique identifier

commands.description: Define an aggregate root that controls access to its children
commands.scenario: Define an aggregate root that controls access to its children

commands.command: // Ensure all operations on Order go through the Order aggregate root
commands.description: Ensure aggregate boundaries are consistent

commands.command: // Use methods on the aggregate root to enforce business rules
commands.description: Design aggregates to enforce invariants
Define a repository for accessing aggregates
commands.scenario: Define a repository for accessing aggregates

commands.description: Implement repository methods for CRUD operations
commands.scenario: Implement repository methods for CRUD operations

commands.command: // Repositories should return full aggregate instances
commands.description: Ensure repositories return aggregates

commands.command: // Use Value Objects to replace primitive obsession
commands.description: Instead of using a string for an email, create an Email class that validates the format upon instantiation to ensure domain integrity.

commands.command: // Implement Domain Events to decouple aggregates
commands.description: Publish an event like 'OrderPlaced' when an aggregate state changes, allowing other bounded contexts to react asynchronously.

commands.command: // Use a Factory pattern for complex aggregate creation
commands.description: Encapsulate the logic of creating a complex aggregate with multiple child entities within a dedicated Factory to ensure it always starts in a valid state.

commands.command: // Apply the Specification pattern for complex business rules
commands.description: Encapsulate reusable business validation logic into 'Specification' objects that can be combined using AND/OR/NOT logic.

commands.command: // Distinguish between Domain Services and Application Services
commands.description: Use Domain Services for business logic that doesn't naturally belong to a single entity, and Application Services for orchestration and transaction management.
